query: most important factor in periodontal flap surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications include patient factors that may compromise treatment outcomes or increase the likelihood of complications.  Tobacco smoking is linked to a less successful reduction of probing depths and attachment gain after periodontal surgery and usually results in more significant gingival recession. [20] Diabetes mellitus is associated with a higher risk of postoperative complications such as increased swelling, flap dehiscence, and delayed wound healing. [21] Immunosuppressive medications and conditions may increase the risk of postoperative infections. Medications that interfere with angiogenesis and bone resorption may pose a risk of jaw osteonecrosis, although the specific postoperative risk is unknown. The risk is likely small but not zero in patients taking oral bisphosphonates and more significant in patients who have received intravenous bisphosphonates for cancer treatment. However, patients with medical conditions including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, coagulopathies, and immunosuppression such as HIV can undergo periodontal surgery, provided that glucose levels, cardiovascular function, and relevant laboratory parameters are within safe limits.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: While not precluding periodontal surgery, age could negatively affect treatment as tissue fibroblasts exhibit increasing senescence, and wound healing is slower. This may increase the risk of tissue tears during surgery and bruising from increased blood vessel fragility.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Complications specific to periodontal surgery include the possibility that the surgery may not achieve the desired outcome and the unique risks associated with each type of periodontal surgery. Pocket depth reduction surgeries typically result in some degree of gingival recession, leading to a longer appearance of the teeth and exposed root surfaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Free gingival and autogenous connective tissue grafting may be challenging if the palatal vault is shallow, contains a palatal torus, or features thin palatal tissue. Pedicle flaps are challenging to perform for broad gingival defects and are unlikely to succeed if adjacent gingival tissues are limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- General Postoperative Complications After Periodontal Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative infections following periodontal surgery are rare, with a reported prevalence of 2%. [83] Antibiotic coverage is generally not necessary after gingival flap surgery, osseous surgery, pedicle flap surgery, or connective and free gingival graft surgery. Antibiotics are typically prescribed after bone grafting and guided tissue regeneration procedures, but the optimal duration of antibiotic therapy for these surgeries lacks consensus or evidence. Generally, patients who use tobacco or have uncontrolled diabetes mellitus are considered to have a higher risk for postoperative infections. Individuals with a history of bisphosphonates and other anti-angiogenic medications are likely at similar risk for medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw as with extractions, although no data is available.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Connective Tissue Grafts and Acellular Dermal Matrix Grafts
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival recession defects Increase gingival or mucosal tissue thickness

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival flap procedures are typically employed when there is residual pocketing without an underlying bone defect, except for generalized bone loss as in suprabony pockets. Alternative names for gingival flap procedures are "open flap debridement" or "surgical scaling and root planing." Widman was one of the first surgeons to describe a gingival flap for pocket reduction. [27] (see Box 2)

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Microvascular Considerations for Periodontal Surgery Flap Design
retrieved_docs.content: Interproximal tissue is at the end of the blood supply. Envelope incisions result in limited capillary bleeding and are preferred for simple periodontal pocket reduction. Vertical-releasing incisions tend to cut superficial mucosal vessels, increasing intraoperative bleeding. By identifying superficial blood vessels and using curved vertical releases parallel to mucosal blood vessels, bleeding from the releasing incision and scarring can be reduced. As facial tissue is the thinnest and has the least blood supply, vertical releases should not be placed over facial mucosa. If surgical access requires a rectangular flap with 2 vertical incisions, the releases should be parallel to the mucosal blood vessels, leaving a broader base for the flap relative to the coronal flap edge.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of periodontal surgery can be categorized into two groups: those common to any oral surgical procedure and those specific to periodontal surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The modified Widman flap is frequently used as a control treatment in clinical trials of periodontal surgery techniques. It differs from a conventional gingival flap in that incisions are kept close to the root surface, and tissue is only reflected enough to allow access to the root surface. Conceptually, the opposite of a modified Widman flap is the apically positioned flap, which features much flap reflection and suturing of the flap margins apical to the alveolar crest by suturing it to the vestibular periosteum. The apically positioned flap may be used with osseous surgery to achieve minimal interdental thickness and maximized probing depth reduction. Unlike the modified Widman flap, the apically positioned flap is associated with significant generalized gingival recession.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal treatment, including periodontal surgery, reduces the rates of tooth loss. [84] [85] After 5 years or longer, desired clinical outcomes such as probing depth reduction and prevention of further attachment loss converge to similar levels for meticulously maintained patients after non-surgical and surgical therapies. [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal surgery should be performed after initial scaling and root planing as it will significantly reduce probing depth and attachment level gain more than periodontal surgery without initial scaling and root planing. [87]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Technique or Treatment -- Flaps for Surgical Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Technique A horizontal incision is made along the gingival margin, extending to adjacent teeth if necessary. The mucoperiosteal flap is then elevated using a periosteal elevator, exposing the surgical site. The flap is repositioned and secured with interrupted sutures after the procedure. Risks The envelope flap is not ideal for exposing large areas or multiple teeth. This flap may lead to a deeper probing depth, as its design restricts thorough postoperative cleaning of the area. A risk of flap tearing during retraction is present. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Lateral Sliding / Pedicle Flap
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of single localized gingival recession or mucogingival defect with a normal amount of keratinized gingiva adjacent to the defect

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Bone grafts, Guide Tissue Regeneration, and Biologics
retrieved_docs.content: Regenerative procedures, including bone grafting, guided tissue regeneration, and biologics, can be implemented to promote the regrowth of lost bone, periodontal ligament, and cementum in residual pockets with deep localized bone defects. Regenerative approaches can fill bone defects, increase clinical attachment, and reduce pockets and gingival recession. However, regeneration success depends on surgeon experience, defect morphology, root surface abnormalities, flap thickness, tobacco use, and medical conditions like diabetes mellitus. [37] In cases where the defect morphology or patient characteristics are unfavorable, surgeons may choose to extract teeth or opt for osseous surgery instead.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 784
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to multiple open wounds that have not yet resulted in additional problems, and it is also associated with open wounds of the extremities. This relationship underscores that even when open wounds—particularly numerous ones or those located on the limbs—have not produced further complications, they are still considered relevant to the broader concept of complication in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section is clinically associated with the low transverse cesarean section technique, and the low transverse cesarean section is in turn clinically associated with early membrane rupture (تمزق الأغشية الباكر). This relationship highlights that the specific low transverse incision used during a cesarean delivery may be linked to an increased likelihood of premature rupture of the fetal membranes, a factor that clinicians consider when planning surgical approaches and managing obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical trachelotomy is linked to a preventive incision, indicating that the procedure incorporates a preventive incision to mitigate potential complications. This relationship underscores the importance of incision planning in obstetrical trachelotomy as a strategy to reduce procedural risks.
